primary_diagnosis.name: Severe hypovolaemic shock secondary to acute (viral) gastroenteritis, with pre-renal acute kidney injury and hypoactive delirium in a frail older adult

pathophysiology_summary: Acute gastroenteritis (watery diarrhoea plus intractable vomiting for 24 hours) has caused massive enteral fluid loss that the non-functioning oral route cannot replace. In a frail 85-year-old with limited physiological reserve this produces profound intravascular volume depletion: postural then frank hypotension, compensatory tachycardia, delayed capillary refill and a weak thready pulse. Falling mean arterial pressure reduces renal perfusion, causing pre-renal acute kidney injury with oliguria/anuria, and cerebral hypoperfusion plus dehydration produce hypoactive delirium (new confusion). This is decompensated hypovolaemic (distributive-adjacent) shock. Definitive treatment is prompt restoration of circulating volume with intravenous crystalloid (the enteral route has failed), which reverses the hypotension, restores renal perfusion and clears the delirium. Withholding the ACE inhibitor (perindopril) is correct because it would worsen hypotension and nephrotoxicity in the volume-depleted state.

differential.evidence_basis: 24h vomiting/diarrhoea, unable to retain oral fluids, dizziness on sitting, anuria and new confusion in a frail elder — classic decompensated volume depletion.
Hypotension, tachycardia and confusion could be septic; must be actively excluded even though a primary GI illness explains the fluid loss.
Vomiting warrants consideration, but active diarrhoea and a soft non-distended abdomen argue against it.
Tachycardia and hypotension in an older adult raise ACS; no chest pain or dyspnoea and haemodynamics fully explained by volume loss make it unlikely but not dismissable remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The administrative title ('IV Therapy — Iron, Fluids, Antibiotics') must not anchor the reasoning; the presented physiology is emergency hypovolaemic shock.

differential.diagnosis: Hypovolaemic shock from gastroenteritis with pre-renal AKI
differential.evidence_basis: Anuria + confusion + postural collapse + inability to retain fluids confirm decompensated shock with end-organ effects; requires urgent parenteral rehydration.
No high fever and no respiratory/urinary localising features; primary GI losses explain the picture, but frail elders can be septic without fever — keep on the list until in-person bloods.
Active diarrhoea, hyperactive bowel sounds and a soft, non-distended, non-guarded abdomen without bilious vomiting effectively exclude it.

differential.diagnosis: Severe hypovolaemic shock secondary to acute gastroenteritis, with pre-renal AKI and delirium
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed on in-person assessment: hypotension (BP 85/50), tachycardia (HR 115), delayed capillary refill, dry mucous membranes, oliguria and confusion — reversed by IV crystalloid resuscitation.

red_flags.description: Anuria / no urine output for 12 hours — established pre-renal acute kidney injury
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals critical renal hypoperfusion; without prompt volume restoration progresses to acute tubular necrosis and irreversible renal damage.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed rehydration risks established acute renal failure requiring intensive care or dialysis.

red_flags.description: New confusion / altered mental state (hypoactive delirium) in a frail elder
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marker of cerebral hypoperfusion and decompensation; altered mentation plus shock mandates emergency-level assessment and precludes safe self-transport.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognising delirium as 'just being unwell' leads to under-triage and preventable deterioration.

red_flags.description: Postural collapse — dizziness/near-syncope on sitting, now bedbound, in a normally mobile patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: Reflects severe orthostatic hypotension from volume depletion; high falls and syncope risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised orthostatic collapse risks a catastrophic fall and fracture.

red_flags.description: Complete intolerance of the oral route (intractable vomiting) — enteral rehydration has failed
red_flags.clinical_significance: Means oral/subcutaneous fluids cannot correct the deficit quickly enough; parenteral (IV) rehydration is required, which telehealth cannot deliver.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Persisting with oral fluid advice alone allows ongoing decompensation.

red_flags.description: New severe localised abdominal pain (screened negative now) — would signal a surgical abdomen
red_flags.clinical_significance: If it appears, points away from simple gastroenteritis toward obstruction/ischaemia/perforation and mandates emergency surgical assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Taking the encounter title 'IV Therapy (Iron, Fluids, Antibiotics)' at face value as a routine infusion/antibiotic visit and under-triaging, instead of reading the vitals and recognising decompensated hypovolaemic shock.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Minting an antibiotic or iron infusion
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being cued by the title into recommending antibiotics (viral gastroenteritis needs none) or an iron infusion (not indicated here) — an error of commission and outside the AI's remit.
Advising continued small sips of oral fluid as the primary plan when the oral route has completely failed and the patient is already shocked, confused and anuric — this is dangerous under-action.
Attempting to direct remote/home IV cannulation and fluid administration; Breath-Ezy cannot perform the procedure and must instead escalate for urgent in-person parenteral rehydration.
Frail elders can be septic without fever; attributing everything to dehydration and not flagging the need for in-person bloods/assessment risks missing a second driver.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Actual blood pressure and heart rate (clinician-measured: BP 85/50, HR 115)
Capillary refill time and pulse character on examination
Degree of mucous-membrane dryness and skin turgor on examination
Serum urea, electrolytes and creatinine to confirm and grade acute kidney injury
Peripheral IV cannulation and any parenteral therapy
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise life-threatening dehydration/hypovolaemic shock with confusion and anuria in a frail elder; arrange an emergency ambulance (000) transfer of the resident to the Emergency Department for urgent IV rehydration and bloods, and instruct the aged-care nursing staff to keep her lying flat, not to let her stand or walk, and to continue withholding perindopril. The AI cannot cannulate or give IV fluids/antiemetics itself.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the broad entity (the dehydration/shock picture is clear); the real risk is UNDER-ACTION — being lulled by the 'IV therapy' title into a routine same-day GP disposition, or advising home oral rehydration, instead of emergency escalation for parenteral treatment the AI cannot provide.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (AU): Fluid and electrolyte management / gastroenteritis in older adults
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Where the oral route has failed and the patient is shocked or has AKI, parenteral (IV) rehydration with isotonic crystalloid is required and should be delivered in an appropriately monitored setting.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ANZSGM — acute care of the frail older person
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: New confusion, hypotension and reduced urine output in a frail older adult are red flags mandating urgent medical assessment; withhold nephrotoxic/hypotensive agents (e.g. ACE inhibitors) during acute volume depletion.
